Firstly, the coupling hatches on both units need to be opened.
Firstly, turn on the auxiliaries. This provides power to the train for air conditioning, saloon lights and passenger information display systems.
For the purpose of this training module, some interactive elements are locked.

Get the train to 10 mph (16 km/h)
Handle semi-fast services with scheduled stops at key interchange points like Watford Junction. Learn to manage braking, station approach, and dwell times while maintaining tight intercity schedules.
Immobilise the units here. Another driver will be taking these in service later.
Immobilise the units here. Another driver will be taking these in service later.
Keeping to speed limits is important. If you begin overspeeding, apply braking force by moving the PBC into the braking range.
Lastly, take out the Master Key.
Learn how to drive the Avanti West Coast Class 805.
Leave the cab to check the headlights
Light engine move from Bushbury Jn to Basford Hall
Loaded engineer's train from Stafford to Basford Hall
Look at the headlights to confirm they are illuminated
Look at the tail lights to confirm they are illuminated
Next, insert the Master Key. This unlocks the train's controls.
Next, put the Reverser to the Off position.
Next, shut down the diesel engines.
Now the cab is setup, we can now open the doors. Open the left passenger doors by using the door controls.
Now the engines are started, put the Reverser to Forward so we can get moving.
Now, raise the pantograph. The pantograph is an arm on the roof of the train that raises up to connect to the overhead wires to deliver power to the train.

On level grades you can coast to maintain your speed, reducing energy consumption and stress on the motors. When coasting always remain vigilant of the gradient, as changes could cause the train to speed up or slow down.

On the mainline, the Class 805s are restricted to 110 mph, following the ‘MU’ speeds displayed on speedboards. On the West Coast Mainline, only tilting trains can operate at speeds up to 125 mph due to tight curves.
Operate a Saturday London Euston to Preston service as far as Milton Keynes, taking leisure travellers home after a day in the capital. Engineering work means you'll be on the slows most of the way.
Operate a special charter from Milton Keynes to Wembley, taking football fans to the match for the cup final. Engineering work means a two track railway is in operation.
Operate an early afternoon service out of London Euston bound for Birmingham New Street. Poor weather is causing issues with the overheads, so disruption is likely.
Operate the final leg of a service from Holyhead. A broken down train is causing disruption, and you'll be joining a queue of trains as you approach London Euston.
Operate your first shift driving an Avanti West Coast class 805 from Wembley Carriage Sidings to London Euston. Carry out a cold start and perform a switch over from diesel power to electric power.
Perform a power change here, going from diesel mode to overhead AC mode.
Perform a power change here, going from diesel mode to overhead AC mode.

Step into the cab of the Class 805 Evero and take command of cutting-edge express services on the southern stretch of the West Coast Main Line. From the bustling platforms of London Euston to key stops like Watford Junction and Milton Keynes Central, you’ll operate high-speed intercity runs with tight timetables, changing conditions, and varied service patterns. Whether running non-stop or making strategic stops, your precision and focus are essential as you deliver the future of rail travel today.
Step up to premium express duties with non-stop services and minimal margins for error. Deliver top-tier performance with professional timing and smooth handling to wrap up your journey in style.
Take charge of an early morning Avanti West Coast service bound for Edinburgh. Late running caused by the ever-changing weather conditions means that delays are likely.

Take charge of northbound express services departing from the capital. Navigate the intensity of London Euston’s platforms and handle clean, high-speed runs into the open main line with precision and confidence.
Test the closed door by pressing the Door Open button

The Class 805 features a combined Power Brake Controller (PBC) that has both power and brake ranges to allow the train to accelerate and brake. Move the PBC into the power range so the train can move.

The Class 805s are bi-mode multiple units capable of running on their onboard diesel engines and the 25kV overhead wires. Built in Kasado, Japan by Hitachi Rail - with final assembly taking place in Newton Aycliffe - the units entered service on 2 June 2024.

The Class 805s consist of thirteen 5-car sets, replacing the older Class 221 'Super Voyager' units from 2002. These can be seen on services from London to Birmingham, Chester and North Wales. The units are maintained at Oxley depot near Wolverhampton.
The coupler hatch needs opening on the other unit. Leave this cab; sit in the driver's seat on the other train and open it's coupler hatch.

The two units are now coupled together. Shutdown this cab and move to the front of the train so we can start the passenger duties.
The two units are now uncoupled. This concludes this training module on the Class 805.

Today you'll be learning the basics of the new Class 805 bi-mode electric multiple units. You'll learn how to drive them; change power modes; couple and uncouple two units and perform passenger duties.
Today you're driving 9G25 from London Euston to Birmingham New Street. The weather is suboptimal, and it's likely you will be caught up in disruption.
Today you're operating the first part of 9S44, a service running all the way to Edinburgh. Perform an exterior prep of the unit and then let passengers board.
